title: Burchard of Worms ranks same-sex acts as ordinary sexual sins
description: In his canon-law collection Decretum, Bishop Burchard of Worms classes homosexual acts alongside transgressions such as adultery, concluding they deserve the same penance, typically fasting.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Peter Damian demands harsher church penalties in Liber Gomorrhianus
description: The monk Peter Damian completes the treatise Liber Gomorrhianus, urging stricter punishment of clerics he accuses of failing in their duty against so-called vices of nature.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Two men wed by a priest in the Kingdom of Leon
description: A priest marries Pedro Dias and Muño Vandilas at a chapel in the Kingdom of Leon, in present-day Spain — one of the earliest recorded church weddings of two men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pope Urban II declines to act against Bishop Giovanni of Orleans
description: Ivo of Chartres accuses Archbishop Rodolfo of Tours of arranging the appointment of his lover Giovanni as bishop of Orleans, a liaison the French king openly acknowledged sharing. Pope Urban II takes no action; Giovanni holds the see for nearly forty years while Rodolfo remains a respected figure.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Council of London declares homosexuality sinful
description: A church council in London takes steps to make clear to the English public that homosexuality counts as sin.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Council of Nablus prescribes burning for sodomy
description: King Baldwin II of the crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem convenes the Council of Nablus, which rules that people who repeatedly commit sodomy are to be burned.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gratian brands sodomy the gravest sexual sin
description: The monk Gratian compiles the canon-law collection Concordia discordantium canonum, arguing that sodomy outranks all other sexual sins because it puts the body to an unnatural use.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Aelred of Rievaulx writes On Spiritual Friendship
description: The English monk Aelred of Rievaulx completes De spiritali amicitia, a work that gives deep expression to love between people of the same gender.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Third Lateran Council orders excommunication of sodomites
description: The Third Lateran Council, meeting in Rome, issues a decree excommunicating people found guilty of sodomy — church-wide legislation with effect across Latin Christendom.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Inquisition begins pursuing sodomy in the Italian city-states
description: Pope Gregory IX launches the Inquisition in the Italian city-states. Several cities punish first and second sodomy convictions with banishment or amputation, and habitual or third-time offenders with burning.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: France imposes mutilation then death for same-sex acts
description: French law of this period punishes a first sodomy conviction with castration, a second with further mutilation, and a third with burning. Women convicted of same-sex acts likewise face mutilation or execution.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Thomas Aquinas ranks sodomy just below bestiality among sins of lust
description: In his theological writing, Thomas Aquinas argues that among the sins of lust only bestiality is worse than sodomy — a ranking that shaped centuries of Catholic moral teaching.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Coutumes de Beauvaisis adds property forfeiture to burning for sodomy
description: The French legal compilation Coutumes de Beauvaisis states that convicted sodomites are to be burned and that their property falls to the crown.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Philip IV destroys the Templars using sodomy charges
description: King Philip IV of France has the Knights Templar arrested on charges of heresy, idolatry and sodomy, a pretext for seizing the order's wealth. The order's leaders are burned at the stake near Notre-Dame in Paris on 18 March 1314.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dante's Inferno consigns sodomites to the Seventh Circle
description: Dante Alighieri's Inferno places sodomites in the Seventh Circle of Hell, fixing the association between same-sex acts and damnation in one of the most influential works of European literature.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Deposed King Edward II of England is killed
description: Edward II, deposed as king of England, is put to death — by later accounts with a heated poker forced into his rectum. His reign had been marked by conflict with nobles who repeatedly exiled his former lover Piers Gaveston, Earl of Cornwall.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rolandino Roncaglia tried for sodomy in Italy
description: The trial of Rolandino Roncaglia causes a sensation in Italy. Roncaglia testified to having no desire for women, lived and worked as a woman after being widowed, and described being widely taken for one — a rare documented case of gender nonconformity in medieval court records.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Thai temple murals depict same-sex relationships
description: Buddhist temple murals showing same-sex relationships are commissioned and painted in Thailand.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: King Gongmin, known for male lovers, takes the Goryeo throne
description: Gongmin, recorded in Korean sources as having male lovers, becomes king of Goryeo in Korea.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jan van Aersdone and Willem Case executed in Antwerp
description: During the 1370s, Jan van Aersdone and Willem Case are executed in Antwerp for same-sex intercourse, then illegal across medieval Europe. Their case is among the few from the era where the names of the accused survive, alongside Giovanni Braganza and Nicoleto Marmagna of Venice.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: John Rykener arrested in London for cross-dressing
description: John Rykener, also called Eleanor, a sex worker active around Cheapside in London and in Oxford, is arrested for cross-dressing and interrogated — one of the few surviving medieval English records touching on gender nonconformity and sex work.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bernardino of Siena preaches against homosexuality in Florence
description: Bernardino of Siena spends three days preaching in Florence against homosexuality and other perceived vices, ending with a bonfire of cosmetics and finery. He calls for sodomites to be cast out of society; his sermons harden public opinion and push the authorities toward tougher persecution.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Texcoco makes homosexuality a capital offense
description: Nezahualcoyotl, ruler of Texcoco in the Valley of Mexico, enacts laws punishing homosexuality with death by hanging.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Florence creates the Night Officials to prosecute sodomy
description: Florence establishes the Night Officials, the first body created specifically to prosecute sodomy. Over the following seventy years the office arrests around 10,000 men and boys and secures roughly 2,000 convictions, most ending in fines.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Royal Noble Consort Sun banished from the Joseon court
description: Royal Noble Consort Sun is expelled from the Joseon court in Korea after her relationship with her maid comes to light. The official decree instead cites receiving visitors without her husband's permission and having her maids sing men's songs.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pope Nicholas V authorizes the Inquisition to pursue sodomy
description: Pope Nicholas V empowers the papal Inquisition to persecute men who practice sodomy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Inca ruler Topa Inca Yupanqui persecutes homosexuals
description: According to Garcilaso de la Vega's chronicle of the Incas, Sapa Inca Topa Inca Yupanqui persecutes homosexuals during his reign. His general Auqui Tatu burns people alive in the Acari valley on even circumstantial evidence of sodomy, while in Chincha the ruler orders mass burnings and the destruction of homes and trees.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Chronicle records burnings of homosexuals in Peru
description: A chronicle written under the Capac Yupanqui government in Peru describes homosexuals being publicly burned and their homes destroyed, a treatment otherwise reserved for conquered tribes.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Leonardo da Vinci twice charged with sodomy and acquitted
description: Florentine court records show Leonardo da Vinci and three other young men facing sodomy charges twice in 1476; both cases end in acquittal.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Spanish Inquisition begins prosecuting sodomy
description: The Spanish Inquisition gets under way, punishing sodomy with stoning, castration and burning. Between 1540 and 1700 more than 1,600 people are prosecuted on sodomy charges.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Erasmus writes love letters to a fellow monk
description: While living at the monastery of Steyn in the Netherlands, Desiderius Erasmus writes a series of love letters to another monk.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Inca ruler Huayna Capac tolerates open same-sex practice
description: Garcilaso de la Vega's chronicle relates that during his reign Sapa Inca Huayna Capac merely asked the people of Tumbez to give up sodomy and took no measures against the Matna, described as practicing it more openly than any other tribe.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Savonarola rails against homosexuality in Florence
description: Girolamo Savonarola berates the people of Florence for what he calls their horrible sins, chiefly homosexuality and gambling, urging them to abandon their young male lovers.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Spain extends and hardens its sodomy laws
description: Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile extend sodomy laws previously limited to the cities across Spain. The offense is elevated to the level of treason or heresy, the evidence needed for conviction is lowered, torture is allowed to extract confessions, and the property of the convicted is confiscated.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sandro Botticelli accused of sodomy
description: A sodomy charge is brought against the Florentine painter Sandro Botticelli.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Zhengde Emperor linked to a same-sex relationship
description: The Zhengde Emperor of Ming-dynasty China is said to have had a same-sex relationship with the Muslim leader Sayyid Husain during his reign, though Chinese sources contain no evidence supporting the claim.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Compagnacci faction revolts in Florence
description: The Compagnacci, the Florentine faction that had opposed Savonarola's moral crackdown — including its campaign against sodomy — stage a revolt in Florence.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Balboa has forty Indigenous men killed as homosexuals
description: The conquistador Vasco Nunez de Balboa, in present-day Panama, is described as throwing forty Indigenous men accused of homosexuality to his dogs.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Magellan imposes the death penalty on crew over a same-sex relationship
description: Ferdinand Magellan sentences members of his own crew to death at Rio de Janeiro after judging them to have had a homosexual relationship.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Benvenuto Cellini first charged with sodomy
description: The Florentine artist Benvenuto Cellini faces the first of several sodomy charges brought against him over his lifetime.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Babur, first Mughal emperor, remembered for his love of Baburi Andijani
description: Babur establishes the Mughal Empire in South Asia. His memoirs record his long attachment to Baburi Andijani, a young man he loved before rising to power.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Holy Roman Empire makes sodomy a capital crime
description: The Holy Roman Empire adopts law making sodomy punishable by death, extending capital punishment for same-sex acts across its territories in central Europe.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Michelangelo begins his love poems to Tommaso dei Cavalieri
description: The Florentine artist Michelangelo starts writing what grows to more than 300 love poems dedicated to Tommaso dei Cavalieri.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Buggery Act makes male homosexuality a capital crime in England
description: Under Henry VIII the Buggery Act of 1533 made sexual acts between men punishable by death in England. It was the first secular law in the country to criminalise such acts and shaped later legislation across Britain and its colonies.

sources.label: High Speed Training — History of LGBTQ+ Timeline
National Museums Liverpool — LGBTQ+ timeline
Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Cabeza de Vaca records same-sex marriages among Native Americans
description: Spanish explorer Alvar Nunez Cabeza de Vaca published an account of his 1528-1536 journey across North America that described same-sex marriages and men living in women's roles among a Native American tribe, an early European record of Indigenous gender diversity.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: England's buggery law is extended to Wales
description: Henry VIII gave royal assent to the Laws in Wales Act 1542, which brought Wales under English law and thereby extended the capital offense created by the Buggery Act 1533 to Welsh territory.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mary I repeals Henry VIII's laws, including the Buggery Act
description: On taking the English throne, Mary I revoked the legislation enacted under Henry VIII during the Reformation of the 1530s. The repeal swept away the Buggery Act 1533, temporarily removing the secular death penalty for sodomy in England.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Elizabeth I restores the Buggery Act in England
description: Between 1558 and 1563, Elizabeth I reinstated the laws of Henry VIII that Mary I had repealed, among them the Buggery Act 1533. Sodomy again became a capital offense under English secular law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Wojciech of Poznan is burned for 'crimes against nature' in Poland
description: Wojciech z Poznania, regarded publicly as a woman, had married Sebastian Slodownik in Poznan and later Wawrzyniec Wloszek in Krakow. In 1561 Wojciech was tried and burned at the stake for 'crimes against nature'.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Spanish colonial authorities execute a man in Florida for sodomy
description: In 1566 Spanish colonial authorities in Florida put a Frenchman to death for homosexual acts, recorded as the earliest death sentence for same-sex activity in what is now the United States.

sources.label: American Psychological Association — LGBTQ history

title: The Boxer Codex describes same-sex marriage in the pre-colonial Philippines
description: The Boxer Codex, a Spanish manuscript from around 1590, recorded that same-sex marriage was a normalized practice in the pre-colonial Philippines.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hu Tianbao is executed in Fujian and later deified as Tu'er Shen
description: During the 17th century, Chinese authorities executed Hu Tianbao of Fujian province. Local people afterwards venerated him as Tu'er Shen, the rabbit god of love between men, and built a temple in his honor.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sweden introduces its first secular law against male sodomy
description: King Charles IX introduced Sweden's first secular statute against male sodomy in 1608. The prescribed punishment was death.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Virginia Colony briefly makes male sodomy a capital offense
description: The Colony of Virginia enacted a military order in 1610 that criminalized male sodomy with the death penalty. The order lapsed later that year when martial law ended after control of the colony changed hands.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)
Medium — A Comprehensive Queer History Timeline of the United States

title: Brandenburg-Prussia makes sodomy punishable by death
description: In 1620 Brandenburg-Prussia criminalized sodomy and set death as the punishment.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Richard Cornish executed for sodomy in the Virginia Colony
description: Richard Cornish of the Virginia Colony was tried and executed for sodomy, one of the earliest recorded capital sodomy cases in colonial America.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History
Medium — A Comprehensive Queer History Timeline of the United States
Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Massachusetts Bay Colony makes sodomy a punishable crime
description: The Massachusetts Bay General Court declared sodomy, along with fornication, adultery and rape, to be crimes subject to punishment.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Connecticut Colony sodomy law covers sex between women
description: The Connecticut Colony enacted a sodomy statute that also prescribed punishment for sexual acts between women, an unusually explicit reference to lesbian conduct for colonial-era law.

sources.label: Medium — A Comprehensive Queer History Timeline of the United States

title: First criminal trial for homosexuality on Canadian territory
description: A military drummer at the French garrison of Ville-Marie in New France, on the site of present-day Montreal, was sentenced to death by local Sulpician priests in 1648 in the first known criminal trial for homosexuality in what is now Canada. After Jesuits in Quebec City intervened, his life was spared on the condition that he serve as New France's first permanent executioner.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First known prosecution for lesbian activity in North America
description: In March 1648 Sarah White Norman was charged in Plymouth, Massachusetts, with lewd behavior on a bed with Mary Vincent Hammon, the earliest known prosecution for lesbian activity in North America. Hammon, under sixteen at the time, was not prosecuted.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sarah Norman charged in first known lesbian prosecution
description: In Plymouth, Massachusetts, Sarah White Norman was charged with lewd conduct with Mary Vincent Hammon, the earliest documented prosecution for sexual activity between women in North America.

sources.label: Medium — A Comprehensive Queer History Timeline of the United States

title: First recorded conviction of women for same-sex conduct in the colonies
description: Sarah White Norman and Mary Vincent Hammon were charged with lewd behavior together in Plymouth, Massachusetts, in what is regarded as the first conviction of two women for same-sex conduct in colonial America.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Connecticut Colony's sodomy law also punishes lesbian intercourse
description: In 1655 the Connecticut Colony passed a law against sodomy that, unusually for the period, also included punishment for lesbian intercourse.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Virginia Colony recriminalizes sodomy by adopting English common law
description: The Colony of Virginia adopted English common law in 1661, which again made sodomy between men a criminal offense in the colony.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Earliest Ethiopian literary reference to homosexuality between nuns
description: The hagiography The Life and Struggles of Our Mother Walatta Petros, completed in 1672, contains the first known reference to homosexuality between nuns in Ethiopian literature.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Denmark makes 'relations against nature' punishable by death
description: In 1683 the Kingdom of Denmark criminalized 'relations against nature' and prescribed the death penalty for the offense.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kagemachaya, an early Japanese gay bar, opens
description: Between 1688 and 1704, during Japan's Genroku era, the Kagemachaya opened as an early Japanese establishment serving men seeking male company, described as the country's first gay bar.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sodomy laws entrenched across the American colonies
description: By this period sodomy statutes were established throughout the early American colonies and the colonial militia, criminalizing same-sex acts.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Catharina Margaretha Linck is executed for female sodomy
description: Catharina Margaretha Linck, who had lived as a man and married a woman, was executed for female sodomy in Germany in 1721 — one of the last documented executions of a woman for this offense in Europe.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Police raid Mother Clap's molly house in London
description: In 1726 police raided Mother Clap's molly house, a London meeting place for men who had sex with men. The raid led to trials and the execution of three men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Utrecht sodomy trials begin in the Netherlands
description: A wave of sodomy prosecutions that started in Utrecht in 1730 spread across the Dutch Republic, leading to the execution of more than a hundred men and the banishment of hundreds more.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Early printed use of the word lesbian by William King
description: The English writer William King used the term lesbian in his satirical work The Toast, an early appearance of the word in print.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: First documented U.S. military discharge for homosexuality
description: Lieutenant Gotthold Frederick Enslin was dismissed from the Continental Army for homosexuality, the first documented case of a military discharge for that reason in American history.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jefferson proposes replacing death penalty for sodomy in Virginia
description: Thomas Jefferson proposed a Virginia law that would punish sodomy by mutilation instead of death; the Virginia legislature rejected the measure.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jens Andersson is tried in Norway after marrying as a man
description: Jens Andersson, assigned female at birth but living as a man, was imprisoned and put on trial in Norway in 1781 after marrying Anne Kristine Mortensdotter in a Lutheran church. Asked about his gender, Andersson answered that he believed he belonged to both.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jeremy Bentham argues for decriminalizing sodomy in England
description: Philosopher Jeremy Bentham wrote in 1785 that sodomy should no longer be a crime in England, making him among the first thinkers to argue for its decriminalization. His essay remained unpublished during his lifetime.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Prince Kraison becomes the first openly queer member of the Chakri dynasty
description: Prince Kraison of Thailand, son of Rama I, was the first openly queer member of the Chakri dynasty and maintained loving relationships with the theatre actors Khun Thong and Yaem.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Death of Frederick the Great of Prussia
description: King Frederick the Great of Prussia, whose sexuality has been the subject of extensive historical debate and who is widely discussed as having loved men, died in 1786.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Equiano narrative references same-sex relationships in slave culture
description: Olaudah Equiano, a formerly enslaved man, published an autobiographical narrative that has been read as describing same-sex relationships within enslaved communities.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Dream of the Red Chamber is published with an openly bisexual character
description: Cao Xueqin's novel Dream of the Red Chamber was published in China in 1791. The book features an openly bisexual character and an account of an attack on a man for his same-sex relations.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: France becomes the first West European country to decriminalize sodomy
description: The French Penal Code of 1791 omitted sodomy from its list of offenses, making France the first West European country to decriminalize homosexual acts between consenting adults. The code also applied in Andorra and in French colonies including Haiti.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Monaco decriminalizes sodomy
description: In 1793 Monaco removed sodomy from its criminal law, following the decriminalization model of revolutionary France.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Luxembourg decriminalizes sodomy
description: Luxembourg removed sodomy from its criminal law in 1794, adopting the decriminalization introduced by the French Penal Code of 1791.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Prussia abolishes the death penalty for sodomy
description: The Kingdom of Prussia ended capital punishment for sodomy in 1794, replacing execution with lesser penalties while keeping the act itself criminal.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Belgium decriminalizes sodomy
description: In 1795 sodomy ceased to be a crime on the territory of present-day Belgium, which came under French law and its 1791 Penal Code after annexation by revolutionary France.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Birth of Swiss folk poet Jakob Stutz
description: Jakob Stutz, later known as a folk poet and school reformer, is born in Switzerland. Throughout his life he wrestled privately with his attraction to men in an era that criminalized it.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Last state execution for male sodomy in continental Europe
description: The Batavian Commonwealth (in present-day Netherlands) carries out continental Europe's last recorded state-sanctioned execution of a man for same-sex sodomy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Charity Bryant and Sylvia Drake begin their partnership in Vermont
description: Vermont residents Charity Bryant and Sylvia Drake enter a lifelong relationship, one of the earliest well-documented same-sex couples in United States history, later chronicled by historian Rachel Hope Cleves.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Duchy of Warsaw re-legalizes same-sex relations
description: With the creation of the Duchy of Warsaw, French-modelled law comes into force on Polish territory, making same-sex sexual activity legal again.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Netherlands decriminalizes homosexual conduct
description: The Netherlands abolishes its laws against homosexual conduct, ending criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kingdom of Bavaria decriminalizes consensual same-sex acts
description: The Kingdom of Bavaria repeals its laws against homosexual conduct between consenting adults, one of the earliest German states to do so.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Term "crime against nature" enters a US criminal code
description: The phrase "crime against nature" appears for the first time in a criminal code in the United States, establishing wording later used to prosecute same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Same-sex relations re-criminalized in Prussian-annexed Poland
description: After the Duchy of Warsaw is partitioned between Prussia and the Russian Empire, Prussian law re-criminalizes same-sex sexual activity in the territory Prussia annexed.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dominican Republic decriminalizes homosexuality
description: The Dominican Republic removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: El Salvador decriminalizes homosexuality
description: El Salvador removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Attack on the Marquis de Custine forces him into the open
description: The Marquis de Custine is beaten and left for dead in Saint-Denis after propositioning a soldier. The resulting scandal exposes his sexuality publicly; he goes on to live openly with his partner Edward St. Barbe while keeping an active social life in Paris.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Empire of Brazil decriminalizes homosexuality
description: The Empire of Brazil removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts with its new criminal code.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bolivia decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Bolivia removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Russian Empire criminalizes sex between men under Article 995
description: The Russian Empire outlaws muzhelozhstvo, interpreted by courts as anal sex between men, under Article 995 of its criminal code. Convicted men lost their legal rights and faced four to five years of exile in Siberia.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Homosexuality criminalized in Congress Poland
description: Congress Poland, the part of Poland under Russian rule after the Partitions, makes homosexuality illegal for the first time in the region's history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: James Pratt and John Smith hanged in Britain's last execution for homosexuality
description: James Pratt and John Smith are hanged at Newgate prison in London after being caught together in private lodgings — the last known execution for homosexuality in Great Britain.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
High Speed Training — History of LGBTQ+ Timeline

title: Heinrich Hössli self-publishes Eros, an early defense of male same-sex love
description: Heinrich Hössli brings out the two volumes of "Eros, die Männerliebe der Griechen" at his own expense, volume one in Glarus, volume two in St. Gallen. The work is among the earliest modern European texts to argue for the acceptance of love between men.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Kingdom of Hannover decriminalizes consensual same-sex acts
description: The Kingdom of Hannover repeals its laws against homosexual conduct between consenting adults.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Birth of Leona Florentino, mother of Philippine lesbian literature
description: Poet Leona Florentino is born in the Spanish-colonized Philippines. Her writing later challenges the colonial patriarchy and lays the foundation of lesbian literature in the archipelago.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Chamberlain and Chaffee begin a lifelong partnership in California
description: Jason Chamberlain and John Chaffee traveled from Boston to California and lived together as lifelong partners in Groveland until Chamberlain's death in 1903.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Men-only miners ball reported in Gold Rush California
description: The traveler J.D. Borthwick described attending a men-only dance, a so-called miners ball, in Angels Camp, California, a glimpse of same-sex social life in Gold Rush mining camps.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Portugal decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Portugal removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Argentina decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Argentina removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Earliest known reference to lesbians in Mormon records
description: A Salt Lake City man's diary notes a Mormon woman "trying to seduce a young girl" — the first known reference to lesbians in Mormon history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: James Buchanan serves as president amid a long same-sex partnership
description: James Buchanan, the only lifelong bachelor to serve as U.S. president, had a long and close relationship with William Rufus King from 1840 to 1853, often cited in histories of same-sex intimacy in American public life.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Ottoman Empire decriminalizes homosexuality
description: During its Tanzimat reform period, the Ottoman Empire — predecessor of modern Turkey — removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: England ends the death penalty for "buggery"
description: The Offences against the Person Act 1861 removes the death sentence for "buggery" in England, a punishment last carried out in 1836. The penalty becomes imprisonment of 10 years to life.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sarah Emma Edmonds serves in the Union Army as Franklin Thompson
description: Sarah Emma Edmonds adopted the identity of Franklin Thompson to enlist in the Union Army, among an estimated 400 documented people assigned female who lived as men to serve in the Civil War.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jennie Hodgers serves three years as Albert Cashier
description: Jennie Hodgers enlisted in the Union Army in Illinois under the name Albert Cashier and fought for three years, continuing to live as a man afterward.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: San Marino decriminalizes homosexuality
description: San Marino removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Karl Heinrich Ulrichs calls for repeal of anti-homosexual laws in Munich
description: Karl Heinrich Ulrichs, widely regarded as the first man to live openly as a homosexual, addresses the Congress of German Jurists in Munich, demanding the abolition of all criminal penalties for homosexual acts. His speech is considered the first public plea of its kind.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Karl Heinrich Ulrichs defends homosexuality before the Congress of German Jurists
description: In Munich, Karl Heinrich Ulrichs pleads before the Congress of German Jurists for a resolution to repeal anti-homosexual laws and is shouted down — the earliest known public self-outing in defence of homosexuality.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Carl von Westphal publishes early clinical writing on same-sex desire
description: In 1869 the German psychiatrist Carl von Westphal published one of the first medical accounts framing same-sex attraction as a clinical subject, an early step in the medicalization of homosexuality in European science.

sources.label: American Psychological Association — LGBTQ history

title: Karl Maria Kertbeny coins the term "homosexual"
description: Hungarian writer Karl Maria Kertbeny uses the word "homosexual" for the first time, in a pamphlet arguing against the criminalization of same-sex acts under Prussian law. The term later becomes the standard word in medicine, law, and everyday speech.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Suriname decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Suriname removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Word "homosexual" appears in print for the first time
description: An anonymous German pamphlet by Austrian-born novelist Karl-Maria Kertbeny, arguing against a Prussian anti-sodomy law, contains the earliest known printed use of the word "homosexual".

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Joseph and His Friend, possibly the first American gay novel, is published
description: Bayard Taylor's Joseph and His Friend: A Story of Pennsylvania appears in print, regarded by some as the first American novel centered on a homosexual relationship.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Guatemala decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Guatemala removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mexico decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Mexico removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Paragraph 175 criminalizes male homosexuality across the German Empire
description: The Reich Criminal Code's Paragraph 175 makes sexual acts between males a crime throughout the newly unified German Empire. The Nazis later broadened the provision in 1935, and thousands of the men prosecuted under it died in concentration camps; the paragraph remained on the books until 10 March 1994.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Death of Charley Parkhurst reveals a life lived as a man
description: Charley Parkhurst, a well-known California stagecoach driver, was found after death to have been assigned female at birth while living as a man throughout adult life.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Empire of Japan decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: The Empire of Japan repeals its ban on anal sodomy between men, a prohibition that had only been introduced in the early Meiji Restoration years.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Paraguay decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Paraguay removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Richard von Krafft-Ebing's sexology reaches medical readers
description: Around 1882 the psychiatrist Richard von Krafft-Ebing's writings on sexual behavior circulated among European medical audiences, shaping how physicians classified homosexuality. The date is approximate and may reflect his later Psychopathia Sexualis.

sources.label: American Psychological Association — LGBTQ history

title: Labouchere Amendment criminalises 'gross indecency' between men
description: Section 11 of the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1885, known as the Labouchere Amendment, made 'gross indecency' between men a crime in Britain. Courts interpreted it broadly to cover almost any intimate contact between men.

sources.label: National Museums Liverpool — LGBTQ+ timeline
Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Henry James novel popularizes the phrase Boston marriage
description: Henry James published The Bostonians, depicting a long-term relationship between two women; the term Boston marriage came into use for such committed female partnerships.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Portugal re-criminalizes homosexual acts
description: Portugal reinstates criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts, reversing its 1852 decriminalization.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: We'wha, a Zuni Two-Spirit, meets the U.S. president
description: We'wha, a Zuni Two-Spirit person, traveled to Washington and met President Grover Cleveland, becoming one of the most documented Two-Spirit figures of the era. The source dates this to 1868, but it is generally placed in 1886.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: We'wha of the Zuni visits Washington and meets President Cleveland
description: We'wha, a lhamana (two-spirit) member of the Zuni tribe, begins a six-month stay in Washington, D.C., during which they call on President Grover Cleveland.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Cleveland Street Scandal breaks in London
description: A police investigation into a male brothel on Cleveland Street in London sets off a public scandal implicating aristocratic clients, fuelling prosecution and public hostility toward men who had sex with men in Victorian Britain.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Birth of Alan Hart, transgender physician and radiology pioneer
description: Alan Hart, later a physician who pioneered the use of X-ray screening for tuberculosis and one of the first documented American transgender men, was born.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Homosexuality legalized on the territory of the Vatican
description: With the Zanardelli Code in force, same-sex acts become legal on the territory of the Vatican, which at the time fell under Italian jurisdiction.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jane Addams founds Hull House alongside partner Mary Rozet Smith
description: Jane Addams established the Hull House settlement in Chicago, supported by her longtime companion and partner Mary Rozet Smith, whose relationship is a notable example of a same-sex partnership in the era.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: The term lesbian enters a medical dictionary
description: The word lesbian appeared in a medical dictionary for the first time, reflecting the growing clinical framing of same-sex desire among women.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: "Bisexual" and "heterosexual" enter English in their modern senses
description: Charles Gilbert Chaddock's English translation of Krafft-Ebing's Psychopathia Sexualis introduces the words "bisexual" and "heterosexual" in the meanings they carry today.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Early printed use of the term bisexual in Psychopathia Sexualis
description: An English translation of the German sexology text Psychopathia Sexualis carried one of the earliest printed uses of the term bisexual.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Birth of bisexual poet Edna St. Vincent Millay
description: Edna St. Vincent Millay, the popular American poet who lived openly as bisexual, is born.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First federal commission on a unified Swiss penal code convenes
description: The Swiss federal councils appoint a first commission to draft a single national penal code. Jacob Rudolf Forster, together with other homosexual men, lobbies the process for impunity for same-sex acts. The codification effort stretches nearly fifty years, until the code takes effect in 1942.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Birth of sexuality researcher Alfred Kinsey
description: Alfred Kinsey, the biologist whose later studies reshaped scientific understanding of human sexuality, is born.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Adolfo Caminha publishes Bom-Crioulo in Brazil
description: Adolfo Caminha's controversial novel Bom-Crioulo (The Black Man and the Cabin Boy) appears in Brazil, placing homosexuality at the center of its story and casting a Black man as its hero.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Oscar Wilde convicted of "gross indecency"
description: Oscar Wilde is prosecuted under the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1885 for "gross indecency" and sentenced to two years of hard labor, a conviction that destroyed his career and became a lasting symbol of Victorian persecution.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Magnus Hirschfeld publishes Sappho and Socrates
description: Magnus Hirschfeld issued the pamphlet Sappho and Socrates, an early defense of same-sex love that anticipated his later work advocating homosexual rights.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Birth of Karl Meier, later founder of Der Kreis
description: Karl Meier is born. Under the pseudonym "Rolf" he later founds and leads the organization KREIS in Zurich along with its trilingual periodical Der Kreis - Le Cercle - The Circle, one of the most influential homophile publications of the mid-20th century.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Havelock Ellis publishes sympathetic studies of homosexuality
description: In 1897 the British writer Havelock Ellis published influential studies that treated homosexuality with relative sympathy while describing same-sex attracted people as a distinct 'third sex', a framing that carried both progressive and pathologizing assumptions.

sources.label: American Psychological Association — LGBTQ history

title: Order of Chaeronea, England's first homosexual rights group, is formed
description: George Cecil Ives organizes the Order of Chaeronea, the earliest known homosexual rights group in England.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Magnus Hirschfeld founds the Scientific-Humanitarian Committee in Berlin
description: Dr. Magnus Hirschfeld establishes the Wissenschaftlich-humanitäres Komitee in Berlin, the world's first organization campaigning for homosexual rights. Its program drew on the earlier writings of Karl Heinrich Ulrichs and Heinrich Hössli.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Magnus Hirschfeld petitions the Reichstag against Paragraph 175
description: Magnus Hirschfeld submits a petition to the Reichstag calling for the abolition of Paragraph 175, opening a repeal campaign he would pursue for the rest of his life.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Adolf Brand launches Der Eigene, the world's first gay periodical
description: Adolf Brand, a member of the Scientific-Humanitarian Committee, begins self-publishing Der Eigene in Berlin, the first magazine in the world aimed at homosexual readers. It appears with some regularity until 1932.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Jacob Rudolf Forster self-publishes a protest against sodomy convictions
description: Jacob Rudolf Forster of St. Gallen publishes his book "Justizmorde im 19. Jahrhundert" in Zurich at his own expense, an outcry against the judicial treatment of homosexual men in 19th-century Switzerland.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1800-1899

title: Honduras decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Honduras removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Liberia decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Liberia repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Swiss Federal Council tasks experts with revising the penal code draft
description: The Federal Council assigns an expert commission to continue work on the new national penal code, building on the proposals of the first commission. The draft's treatment of same-sex acts remains one of the open questions.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Marcela Gracia and Elisa Sánchez marry in A Coruña
description: On 8 June 1901 Marcela Gracia Ibeas and Elisa Sánchez Loriga were wed in A Coruña, Galicia. To make the ceremony possible, Sánchez assumed the male identity of Mario Sánchez, the name entered on the marriage certificate.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Uganda criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Uganda enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Argentina decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Argentina repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Mauritania decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Mauritania repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Preliminary Swiss penal code draft sparks debate on homosexuality
description: The preliminary draft of the new Swiss penal code is presented. Its paragraphs on homosexuality trigger a public discussion about the causes of same-sex attraction.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Thailand criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Thailand enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: New York police raid the Ariston Hotel Baths
description: On 21 February 1903 police raided the Ariston Hotel Baths in New York City, the first recorded raid on a gay bathhouse in the United States. Thirty-four men were arrested, twelve stood trial on sodomy charges, and seven drew prison terms of four to twenty years.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Argentina equalizes the age of consent
description: Argentina set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Detained youths at Les Douaires riot against a same-sex relationship ban
description: In July 1905 young people held at the Les Douaires detention site in France rioted after the penal administration tried to ban homosexual relationships among detainees, an episode counted among the earliest recorded queer riots.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Imre, an early gay novel with a happy ending, is published
description: The novel Imre appeared in 1906 and is regarded as possibly the first openly gay American novel to close on a happy note for its protagonists.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Karl M. Baer undergoes pioneering gender-affirming surgery
description: In December 1906 Karl M. Baer became the first known transgender person to receive sex reassignment surgery.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Adolf Brand jailed after outing Chancellor von Bülow
description: Activist Adolf Brand of the Gemeinschaft der Eigenen, campaigning against Paragraph 175, published a piece in 1907 outing German chancellor Bernhard von Bülow. Von Bülow won a libel suit that cleared his name, while Brand served 18 months in prison.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: China decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: China repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Gertrude Stein meets Alice B. Toklas in Paris
description: Gertrude Stein met Alice B. Toklas in Paris, beginning a lifelong partnership and a salon that drew together many writers and artists of the period.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Harden–Eulenburg affair unfolds in Germany
description: Between 1907 and 1909 a chain of libel trials, courts-martial and press campaigns over alleged homosexuality within Kaiser Wilhelm II's inner circle became known as the Harden–Eulenburg affair, feeding a major public scandal around homosexuality in Germany.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hirschfeld testifies that homosexuality is part of nature
description: At a German trial, Magnus Hirschfeld testified that homosexuality was a natural part of human variation, using the courtroom to advance his scientific and legal arguments.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Emma Goldman speaks publicly for homosexual rights
description: From 1910 the anarchist Emma Goldman defended homosexual love in her public lectures in the United States. Magnus Hirschfeld later credited her as the first woman, indeed the first American, to take up that defense before a general audience.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hirschfeld's study Die Transvestiten coins the term transvestitism
description: Magnus Hirschfeld published Die Transvestiten, a study classifying cross-dressing that introduced the term transvestitism. It was among the earliest medical works to treat gender variance as a distinct subject.

sources.label: Royal Society of Medicine — Snapshots in LGBTQ+ Medical History

title: Paraguay criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Paraguay enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Alexander Berkman publishes Prison Memoirs of an Anarchist
description: Berkman's 1912 memoir treated love between men in prison with unusual openness; historian Terence Kissack ranks it among the most significant American political texts on homosexuality written before the 1950s.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jacob Schorer launches the Dutch Scientific Humanitarian Committee
description: Dr. Jacob Schorer set up the NWHK in 1912, the first organization in the Netherlands to campaign against discrimination toward homosexuals.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mormon journal prints a tribute to Sappho of Lesbos
description: In 1912 the Young Woman's Journal paid tribute to Sappho of Lesbos, the first explicit reference to lesbianism in a Mormon magazine.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Third expert commission drafts a new Swiss penal code
description: A third expert commission takes up the work of producing a fresh draft for Switzerland's unified national penal code, continuing the decades-long codification process that would eventually decriminalize same-sex acts.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Earliest known print use of the word faggot for gay men
description: A 1913 vocabulary of criminal slang published in Portland, Oregon contains the first documented use of the word in print in reference to gay men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Harlem Renaissance becomes a hub of queer cultural life
description: The Harlem Renaissance flourished in New York, a movement historians have described as being as queer as it was Black, with many prominent artists and writers living openly non-heterosexual lives.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Moldova decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Moldova repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: October Revolution voids Russia's sodomy statute
description: The October Revolution of 1917 annulled the old imperial criminal code in its entirety, taking Article 995 against male homosexual acts with it. Bolshevik leaders of the period stated that homosexual and heterosexual relations stood equal before the law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Russia decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Russia repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Moldova equalizes the age of consent
description: Moldova set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Federal Council submits draft for a national Swiss penal code
description: The Swiss Federal Council presents its draft for a unified national penal code (StGB) to parliament. The move opens the political battle over its contested provisions, among them the question of whether homosexual acts should be criminalized nationwide.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Gay film Different from the Others premieres
description: The 1919 German picture Different from the Others, among the first films to portray homosexuality openly, reached cinemas with Magnus Hirschfeld appearing in a cameo and partially financing the production.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hirschfeld founds the Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin
description: Magnus Hirschfeld established the Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin, a pioneering center for research, advocacy and care that became central to the early homosexual rights movement.

title: Canton of Basel-Stadt decriminalizes homosexuality
description: The revised penal law of the canton of Basel-Stadt comes into force, ending the prosecution of homosexual acts in Basel more than two decades before decriminalization applied across Switzerland.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Harlem speakeasies bring gay nightlife to wider audiences
description: During the 1920s, blues performances and drag entertainment in Harlem and New York speakeasies exposed broad Prohibition-era audiences to gay culture. The decade date is approximate.

sources.label: American Psychological Association — LGBTQ history

title: Namibia decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Namibia repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Attempt to criminalise sex between women fails in Britain
description: A proposal to extend the Labouchere Amendment to same-sex relationships between women was dropped by Parliament. Opponents argued that publicising the offence would draw attention to acts most women supposedly knew nothing about.

sources.label: National Museums Liverpool — LGBTQ+ timeline

title: British attempt to criminalize lesbianism fails
description: A 1921 parliamentary move to make sexual acts between women illegal for the first time in Britain's history did not pass.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: BBC founded while sex between men remains illegal in Britain
description: The British Broadcasting Company was established in 1922. At the time sex between men was a criminal offence in the United Kingdom and LGBT people lived largely hidden lives, while there was no specific law addressing relationships between women.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Soviet criminal code confirms decriminalization of homosexual acts
description: A new criminal code that came into force in the USSR in 1922 contained no offense covering consensual homosexual acts, formally decriminalizing them.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First Swiss homosexual organization founded in Lucerne
description: A group of men in Lucerne establishes Switzerland's first homosexual association, renamed within the year to "Schweizer Freundschaftsbund / Société amicale suisse". Its two-day founding meeting marks the start of organized gay life in the country.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Earliest known print use of the word fag for gay men
description: Nels Anderson's 1923 study The Hobo contains the first documented printed use of the term in reference to gay men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Elsa Gidlow publishes the first openly lesbian US poetry volume
description: English-born poet Elsa Gidlow brought out On a Grey Thread in 1923, the first volume of openly lesbian love poetry published in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Henry Gerber founds the first U.S. gay rights organization
description: Henry Gerber established the Society for Human Rights in Chicago, recognized as the first gay rights organization in the United States, though it was quickly suppressed.

title: Paraguay removes criminal penalties for homosexuality
description: Consensual homosexual acts ceased to be a criminal offense in Paraguay, an event this timeline dates to 1924; other references give differing years, so the date merits verification.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Peru decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Peru repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

title: Henry Gerber founds the Society for Human Rights in Chicago
description: On 10 December 1924 Henry Gerber established the Society for Human Rights in Chicago, the earliest documented gay rights organization in the United States. It issued Friendship and Freedom, the first American publication aimed at homosexual readers, but folded soon afterward under political and legal pressure.

title: Schweizer Freundschaftsbund opens its own clubhouse in Zurich
description: The Schweizer Freundschaftsbund moves into premises of its own in Zurich's old town, giving Switzerland's early homosexual movement its first dedicated meeting place.

title: Schweizer Freundschaftsbund renamed Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte
description: At its general assembly in Zurich, the Schweizer Freundschaftsbund adopts the new name "Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte" (Swiss League for Human Rights), reframing the homosexual cause in the language of human rights.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Police raid closes lesbian café Eve's Hangout
description: Eve's Hangout, a well-known lesbian café in Greenwich Village, shut its doors in 1926 following a police raid.

title: Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte fades out of existence
description: The Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte disappears from published records, marking the apparent end of Switzerland's first homosexual organization after roughly four years of activity.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: The New York Times prints the word homosexuality
description: In 1926 The New York Times became the first major publication to use the word homosexuality in its pages.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Karol Szymanowski appointed head of Poland's national music school
description: In 1927 Karol Szymanowski, Poland's openly gay leading composer, took charge of the state-owned Fryderyk Chopin Music Academy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ethel Smyth's music first broadcast by the BBC
description: The composer Ethel Smyth, who had many relationships with women and wrote the suffragette anthem 'March of the Women', had her work broadcast by the BBC and was honoured for her 75th birthday. She was the first woman appointed a Dame for services to music.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Radclyffe Hall publishes The Well of Loneliness
description: Radclyffe Hall published the novel The Well of Loneliness, a landmark work of lesbian literature that provoked obscenity disputes and public debate in the United States and England.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History
Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay policeman Harry Daley broadcasts on BBC radio
description: Harry Daley, a gay London police officer, spoke in the 'My Day's Work' slot on the BBC's Children's Hour. He had been brought to broadcasting by the BBC producer J. R. Ackerley and moved in a literary circle that included E. M. Forster.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Swiss National Council debates penal code draft decriminalizing homosexuality
description: The National Council, the lower chamber of the Swiss parliament, deliberates the draft of the new national penal code, which foresees the removal of criminal penalties for homosexual acts.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Katharine Lee Bates, author of America the Beautiful, dies
description: The American poet Katharine Lee Bates, remembered for writing America the Beautiful, died on 22 May 1929.

title: Mexico decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Mexico repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

title: Reichstag committee votes to strike Paragraph 175
description: On 16 October 1929 a Reichstag committee approved repealing Paragraph 175, the German statute penalizing male homosexuality. The vote was never implemented, as the Nazi rise to power intervened.

title: Dora Richter receives the first known vaginoplasty
description: In Berlin in 1931 Dora Richter became the first transgender woman known to undergo vaginoplasty.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Las Carolinas stage the first documented LGBT demonstration
description: In 1931 a group of Barcelona transvestites known as Las Carolinas protested publicly after the demolition of a central public bath that had served as an LGBT meeting place — regarded as the first documented LGBT demonstration in history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pro-lesbian film Mädchen in Uniform is released
description: The 1931 German picture Mädchen in Uniform became one of the first explicitly lesbian films and the first to present lesbian love sympathetically.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Women's club Amicitia founded in Zurich
description: The Damen-Club "Amicitia" is established in Zurich, creating an early organized meeting place for lesbian women in Switzerland.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Swiss Council of States approves the new penal code
description: Following its deliberations, the Council of States, the upper chamber of the Swiss parliament, votes in favor of the new national penal code, keeping the decriminalization of homosexual acts on track.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Excentric-Club Zürich founded for men
description: The "Excentric-Club Zürich" (E.C.Z.), a social club for homosexual men, is established in Zurich, adding to the city's growing club scene of the early 1930s.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Book and literature club founded in Basel on New Year's Eve
description: On New Year's Eve, the "Bücherfreunde & Literaturclub Basel" is founded, a literary society that served as a discreet gathering point for homosexual men in Basel.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Actor Karl Meier returns from Berlin to Switzerland
description: The actor Karl Meier, later editor of Der Kreis under the name "Rolf", moves back to Switzerland from Berlin, where the political climate for homosexual men was rapidly deteriorating.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: First documented gender reassignment surgery on Dora Richter
description: Doctors Ludwig Levy-Lenz and Erwin Gohrbandt performed the first documented gender reassignment surgery on Dora Richter, who worked at Hirschfeld's Institute for Sexual Science. She became the first transgender woman documented to undergo a vaginoplasty.

sources.label: Royal Society of Medicine — Snapshots in LGBTQ+ Medical History

title: Poland codifies an equal age of consent
description: Poland's 1932 penal code set the age of consent at 15 for homosexual and heterosexual acts alike. Polish law itself had never criminalized homosexuality; occupying powers had outlawed it in 1835.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Short-lived men's club Freundschaftsbund Zürich formed
description: The men's club "Freundschaftsbund Zürich" (FBZ) is formed in Zurich but folds again shortly after its creation.

description: Spain repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

title: Freundschafts-Banner, first Swiss homosexual magazine, appears
description: The first issue of Freundschafts-Banner is published, making it Switzerland's first periodical for homosexual readers and the starting point of a publishing lineage that later produced Der Kreis.

description: Poland repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

description: A new Danish penal law adopted in 1933 removed criminal penalties for consensual homosexual acts.
