title: Nazi Germany imprisons homosexual men in concentration camps
description: Under Nazi rule an estimated 100,000 homosexual men in Germany were arrested and many were sent to concentration camps, where they were forced to wear the pink triangle.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: New club formed under the name Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband
description: Alongside the magazine relaunch, a new club calling itself Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband (SFB) is created. The source record is thin on how this body related to the Amicitia association founded the same month.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Philippine law leaves homosexuality outside the criminal code
description: This timeline records the Philippines as decriminalizing homosexuality in 1933, the period when the country's revised penal code — which contains no offense for consensual same-sex acts — took effect.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Short-lived branches open in Basel and Lucerne
description: Local sections of the Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Bund are set up in Basel and Lucerne, but both dissolve again after a short time.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Soviet decree recriminalizes homosexual acts
description: In 1933 the USSR moved to restore criminal penalties for consensual sex between men, reversing the decriminalization in force since the revolutionary period.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Uruguay decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Uruguay repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Uruguay equalizes the age of consent
description: Uruguay set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Portugal criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Portugal enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Mixed association Amicitia founded in Zurich for women and men
description: The "Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband Amicitia" (S.Fr.V.) is founded in Zurich, open to both women and men — a rarity among the homosexual associations of the period.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Freundschafts-Banner relaunched as Schweizerisches Freundschafts-Banner
description: After the original run of Freundschafts-Banner ended within months in 1932, the magazine returns in Zurich under the new title Schweizerisches Freundschafts-Banner, continuing Switzerland's first homosexual periodical.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Soviet ban on male homosexuality enters the criminal code
description: In 1934 the USSR formally codified the offense of muzhelozhstvo — sex between men — punishable by up to five years in prison, with harsher terms where coercion or minors were involved.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Uruguay decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Uruguay removed consensual same-sex acts between adults from its criminal law in 1934.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Karl Meier applies for club membership as Rudolf Rheiner
description: Using the pseudonym Rudolf Rheiner, Karl Meier applies to join the Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband — the beginning of his involvement with the organization he would later lead as "Rolf".

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Organization renamed Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte
description: The Schweizerischer Freundschaftsbund takes the new name "Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte" (Swiss League for Human Rights), once again presenting the homosexual cause under a human-rights banner.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Colombia criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Colombia enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Mona's 440 Club, first US lesbian bar, opens in San Francisco
description: Mona's 440 Club opened in San Francisco in 1936 as the first lesbian bar in the United States. Its waitresses and female performers wore tuxedos, and patrons dressed to match their roles.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Poet Federico García Lorca is executed
description: Spanish poet and playwright Federico García Lorca was shot in 1936, in the opening phase of the Spanish Civil War.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pink triangle introduced for gay prisoners in Nazi camps
description: From 1937 Nazi concentration camps marked men imprisoned for homosexuality with the pink triangle badge.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Romania criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Romania enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Magazine Freundschafts-Banner renamed Menschenrecht
description: The Swiss homosexual periodical Freundschafts-Banner changes its title to Menschenrecht ("Human Right"), matching its publisher's human-rights framing.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Swiss parliament adopts the national penal code
description: Both chambers of the Swiss federal parliament pass the national penal code (StGB), whose provisions end the criminalization of consensual homosexual acts between adults once the code takes effect.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Douglas Byng is the first female impersonator on television
description: Douglas Byng, a cabaret performer, appeared as the first female impersonator on BBC television. Openly gay within theatrical circles but discreet in public, he went on to host his own programmes.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Ecuador criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Ecuador enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Mass trial of 74 homosexual men in St. Gallen
description: A court in St. Gallen tries 74 homosexual men in a single proceeding, handing down severe sentences — one of the largest prosecutions of homosexual men in Swiss history, on the eve of national decriminalization.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: The word gay is first spoken on film in a homosexual sense
description: In the 1938 comedy Bringing Up Baby, the word gay was used on screen in reference to homosexuality for the first time.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Austria criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Austria enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Swiss voters approve the penal code in a national referendum
description: After opponents forced a referendum, the Swiss electorate votes to accept the new federal penal code, confirming the decriminalization of homosexual acts contained in it.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte holds its final assembly
description: The Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte convenes its last general assembly; the organization is dissolved shortly afterwards.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Diana: A Strange Autobiography breaks ground for lesbian memoir
description: In 1939 Frances V. Rummell, a teacher of French at Stephens College, published Diana: A Strange Autobiography, the first explicitly lesbian autobiography in which two women end up together happily. The publisher added a note vouching that the story was true.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Poland criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Poland enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Iceland decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Iceland abolished criminal penalties for consensual homosexual acts in 1940.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dutch Scientific Humanitarian Committee dissolved amid invasion
description: In May 1940 the NWHK disbanded in the Netherlands as Germany invaded. Members destroyed most of its archive to keep it out of enemy hands, and Nazi soldiers confiscated what remained.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

description: Netherlands enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

description: Belgium enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Bandleader Ken 'Snakehips' Johnson killed in a London air raid
description: Ken 'Snakehips' Johnson, a Guyana-born swing bandleader who broadcast for the BBC and lived with the writer Gerald Hamilton, was killed by a bomb while performing at the Café de Paris in London during the Blitz.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Gay and lesbian Americans enlist after Pearl Harbor
description: Following the attack on Pearl Harbor, large numbers of gay and lesbian Americans joined the military, part of a wartime surge in enlistment reported to reach 14,000 people per day in 1942.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: The term transsexuality sees its first recorded use
description: In 1941 the word transsexuality was used for the first time, at that point still in reference to homosexuality and bisexuality.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Women's pages in Menschenrecht appear as Die Freundin, then cease
description: The women's section of the magazine Menschenrecht is given its own title, Die Freundin, but is discontinued at the end of the year for lack of interest.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Cadet scandal erupts in Buenos Aires
description: In 1942 a scandal involving military cadets and homosexuality broke out in Buenos Aires, turning public and official attention onto gay life in Argentina.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: France criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: France enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Karl Meier takes over Menschenrecht; publisher becomes DER KREIS
description: Karl Meier succeeds Anna Vock as editor of the magazine Menschenrecht, and its publishing house adopts the name DER KREIS — the beginning of the magazine's transformation into the internationally read Der Kreis.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Subscribers form the first Tischrunden DER KREIS gatherings
description: Readers of Menschenrecht begin organizing regular social round tables called "Tischrunden DER KREIS", first in Zurich, then in Bern, Biel, and Chur — the social network that underpinned the Der Kreis community.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Switzerland decriminalizes homosexual acts as the penal code takes effect
description: The new federal penal code comes into force, ending the criminal prosecution of consensual homosexual acts between adults across Switzerland — the culmination of a codification process begun in the 1890s, though the age of consent remained unequal.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Menschenrecht adds a regular French-language section
description: From April onward, the Swiss homosexual magazine Menschenrecht carries a regular French-language section, extending its reach into francophone Switzerland.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1900-1942

title: Der Kreis - Le Cercle launches as a bilingual monthly
description: The magazine Menschenrecht is succeeded by the new bilingual monthly Der Kreis - Le Cercle, distributed through a newly created subscriber organization, the Lesezirkel DER KREIS - LE CERCLE. The publication became the leading homophile magazine of its era in Europe.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1943-1967

title: DER KREIS meeting point opens in Basel
description: A local DER KREIS meeting point is established in Basel, operating with interruptions until the end of 1946.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1943-1967

title: Allied forces liberate Nazi concentration camps
description: United States, British and Soviet forces liberated the people held in Nazi concentration camps, including prisoners persecuted for homosexuality.

title: Belize criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Belize enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: LE CERCLE Lausanne founded, lasts only months
description: LE CERCLE Lausanne, styled "Rendez-vous des camarades", is founded as a French-speaking offshoot of the Der Kreis network, but dissolves after only a few months.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1943-1967

title: Poet Robert Duncan comes out in print
description: Writing under his own name in the anarchist magazine Politics in 1944, poet Robert Duncan described homosexuals as an oppressed minority — making him the first prominent American to reveal his homosexuality publicly.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Short-lived DER KREIS meeting point in Davos
description: A DER KREIS meeting point opens in Davos but survives for only a few months.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1943-1967

title: Suriname records legalization of homosexuality
description: This timeline lists Suriname as legalizing homosexual acts in 1944; other references date the absence of such an offense earlier, so the year merits checking.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sweden decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Sweden lifted its criminal ban on consensual homosexual acts in 1944, fixing the age of consent for them at 18.

description: A DER KREIS meeting point is set up in St. Gallen, remaining active until 1948.

title: Gay bar Yanagi opens in Japan
description: The bar Yanagi, an early gay venue in Japan, opened its doors in 1945.

title: Gay veterans form the Veterans Benevolent Association
description: Four honourably discharged gay servicemen created the Veterans Benevolent Association in 1945, the first LGBT veterans' organization in the United States.

title: Gillies and Millard perform a phalloplasty on Michael Dillon
description: Sir Harold Gillies and Ralph Millard carried out a pedicle-flap phalloplasty on Dr Michael Dillon in the United Kingdom. The reconstructive technique, developed for injured soldiers, was offered as a genital surgery option for transgender men.

sources.label: Royal Society of Medicine — Snapshots in LGBTQ+ Medical History

title: Homosexual prisoners are among the last freed after liberation
description: Men imprisoned for homosexuality were among the last groups released after the liberation of the camps, because Paragraph 175 of the German Criminal Code kept their conduct criminal.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Netherlands decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Netherlands repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Persecution of gay men persists as the Holocaust ends
description: By the end of the Holocaust in 1945, an estimated 3,000 to 9,000 homosexuals had died in Nazi concentration and death camps. An estimated 2,000 to 6,000 gay camp survivors were made to serve out the remainder of their Paragraph 175 sentences in prison.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Queer nightlife returns to Berlin after the war
description: The first gay bar of post-war Berlin opened in the summer of 1945, and that autumn the American sector of West Berlin hosted its first drag ball.

title: Fiji criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Fiji enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: COC, the oldest surviving LGBT organization, forms in the Netherlands
description: The Dutch group COC — its acronym standing for Center for Culture and Recreation — was established in 1946 as one of the earliest homophile organizations and remains the oldest LGBT organization still in existence.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Michael Dillon receives gender-affirming surgery in Britain
description: Plastic surgeon Harold Gillies carried out sex reassignment surgery on Michael Dillon in Britain in 1946.

title: Lisa Ben creates Vice Versa, the first US lesbian publication
description: Between 1947 and 1948 Edith Eyde, writing as Lisa Ben, typed and self-published Vice Versa in Los Angeles, the first American lesbian publication.

description: Taiwan repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

description: Alfred Kinsey published his research on the sexual behaviour of human males and females, criticising the tendency of scientists to portray homosexuals and heterosexuals as inherently different types of people. The work later influenced the reclassification of homosexuality.

description: The group Maricas Unidas Argentinas came together in 1948 and may have been the first LGBTQ organization in Latin America.

title: WHO lists homosexuality as a disease in its international classification

title: Lavender Scare purges begin in the US government

description: On 15 December 1950 a Senate report titled Employment of Homosexuals and Other Sex Perverts in Government told Congress that homosexuals posed security risks. title: Christine Jorgensen's transition draws worldwide attention
description: Christine Jorgensen, a US Army veteran who underwent gender confirmation surgery in Denmark, became the first transgender woman whose transition was widely publicised by the media. She spoke openly about hormone therapy and surgery and became an advocate for transgender people.

title: Dale Jennings fights entrapment charge in Los Angeles
description: Arrested in a police sting at Westlake Park, Dale Jennings contested the solicitation charge with backing from the Mattachine Society. The trial ended in a hung jury, drew national attention, and sharply increased the society's membership.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay magazine Adonis launches in Japan
description: The Japanese magazine Adonis, aimed at homosexual men, began publication in 1952 with the writer Yukio Mishima among its contributors.

title: One, Inc. is established as a homophile organization
description: In 1952 activists established One, Inc. on the West Coast, an early homophile organization that published One magazine and litigated for the right to distribute gay material through the mail.

sources.label: American Psychological Association — LGBTQ history

title: Spring Fire launches the lesbian pulp fiction genre
description: Marijane Meaker, writing under the pen name Vin Packer, published the first lesbian paperback original novel. Spring Fire sold 1.5 million copies and opened the lesbian pulp fiction genre.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: U.S. immigration law bars homosexual entrants
description: Congress passed an Immigration Act barring people with a psychopathic personality, a phrase officials clarified was meant to exclude homosexuals from entering the country.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Actor John Gielgud arrested for a homosexual offence
description: The recently knighted actor John Gielgud was arrested for 'cottaging' in London. The case was one of several high-profile prosecutions of the early 1950s that helped prompt a review of the laws on homosexuality.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Evelyn Hooker wins an NIMH grant to study gay men
description: In 1953 the psychologist Evelyn Hooker received a National Institute of Mental Health grant to study the psychological adjustment of gay men, funding research that would challenge the illness model of homosexuality.

title: Kinsey report documents female homosexual behavior
description: Alfred Kinsey published Sexual Behavior in the Human Female, extending his earlier research to include data on same-sex behavior among women.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Diana Foundation founded in Houston
description: A small circle of friends in Houston started the Diana Foundation on 19 March 1953. It is regarded as the oldest continuously active gay organization in the United States and stages annual fundraisers including the Diana Awards.

title: Executive Order 10450 bars gay people from US federal jobs
description: Amid the Lavender Scare that ran alongside McCarthy's anti-communist campaign, President Eisenhower signed an executive order excluding homosexuals from federal employment. Thousands of workers lost their jobs over actual or suspected sexual orientation.

title: Alan Turing dies after conviction for homosexuality
description: The mathematician and computing pioneer Alan Turing died from cyanide poisoning, recorded as suicide. description: On 21 September 1955 the Daughters of Bilitis was established in San Francisco as the first lesbian rights organization in the United States. It offered social gatherings as a safer alternative to bars and clubs, which were frequently raided by police.

title: Evelyn Hooker's study challenges pathologisation of homosexuality
description: Psychologist Evelyn Hooker published an influential paper showing that homosexual men are as psychologically well-adjusted as heterosexual men. The finding became a major factor in later reclassification of homosexuality by psychiatry.

sources.label: Royal Society of Medicine — Snapshots in LGBTQ+ Medical History

title: Evelyn Hooker shows gay men are as well-adjusted as others
description: In 1956 Evelyn Hooker presented research finding no difference in psychological adjustment between gay and heterosexual men, providing key scientific evidence against classifying homosexuality as a mental disorder.

sources.label: American Psychological Association — LGBTQ history

title: Florida's Johns Committee begins targeting homosexuals
description: The Florida Legislative Investigation Committee, known as the Johns Committee, was set up in 1956. After failing to link civil rights groups to communism, it turned to investigating homosexuals as an alleged threat to national security.

title: Rina Natan becomes Israel's first transgender woman to obtain surgery
description: Rina Natan was the first transgender woman in Israel to undergo sex reassignment surgery of her own volition. The source dates this only to the 1950s; 1956 is the year most often cited.

description: Thailand removed criminal penalties for consensual homosexual acts in 1956.

title: Barfüsser opens in Zurich's Niederdorf
description: In August 1956 the cellar bar Barfüsser opened in Zurich's Niederdorf quarter. It went on to serve as a popular gay and lesbian meeting place for many years.

title: Evelyn Hooker's research challenges the disease model of homosexuality
description: On 30 August 1956 psychologist Evelyn Hooker presented findings arguing that homosexuality was not a clinical disorder and that homosexual and heterosexual men showed no significant psychological differences. Her work helped shift clinical thinking away from treating homosexuality as an illness.

description: The Zurich homophile organization Der Kreis celebrated its 25th anniversary in 1957, a quarter century after its beginnings in the early 1930s.

title: Evelyn Hooker publishes study on gay men's mental health
description: Psychologist Evelyn Hooker showed that homosexual men were as psychologically well-adjusted as heterosexual men. Her findings became a key argument for the American Psychiatric Association's 1973 removal of homosexuality from its list of disorders.

title: Frank Kameny dismissed from federal service for homosexuality

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Harry Benjamin coins the term transsexual
description: Endocrinologist and sexologist Harry Benjamin introduced the term transsexual, distinguishing the concept from transvestitism. His clinical approach later took precedence over psychoanalytic treatment.

description: In 1965 Frank Kameny and Barbara Gittings led public gay rights demonstrations in Philadelphia and Washington, D.C., among the first organized protests of their kind in the United States.

title: Politicians face questions at first gay-vote event
description: The Council on Religion and the Homosexual hosted a 1965 forum where local politicians answered questions on issues affecting gay and lesbian people, including police intimidation. It is considered the first recorded courting of the gay vote.

description: LGBT youth in San Francisco's low-income Tenderloin district created Vanguard in 1965, regarded as the first gay liberation organization in the United States.

title: Polari reaches millions through Julian and Sandy on the BBC
description: The radio comedy 'Round the Horne' introduced the camp characters Julian and Sandy, played by Hugh Paddick and Kenneth Williams, who spoke the gay slang Polari to millions of Sunday listeners.

title: First Annual Reminder picket at Independence Hall
description: On 4 July 1965 conservatively dressed gay men and lesbians picketed outside Independence Hall in Philadelphia. The demonstration opened the series of Annual Reminders held each year through 1969.

description: Algeria enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

description: The Beaumont Society was established as a support group for transvestite and transgender people, becoming one of the earliest such organisations in Britain.

description: Transgender people and drag queens fought back against police harassment at Compton's Cafeteria in San Francisco, an uprising that helped lead to the National Transsexual Counseling Unit.

description: The Society for Individual Rights opened America's first gay and lesbian community center in San Francisco in 1966.

title: Harry Benjamin publishes The Transsexual Phenomenon
description: Harry Benjamin published The Transsexual Phenomenon, which separated transvestitism from transsexuality and argued for acceptance of gender-affirming surgery. The book shaped clinical treatment of transgender people for decades.

sources.label: Royal Society of Medicine — Snapshots in LGBTQ+ Medical History

title: Los Angeles motorcade protests military exclusion
description: For Armed Forces Day 1966, a coalition of homophile groups demonstrated against the exclusion of LGBT people from the US armed services. The Los Angeles group's 15-car motorcade has been described as the nation's first gay pride parade.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: North American homophile groups form national conference
description: The National Planning Conference of Homophile Organizations was established in 1966, becoming the North American Conference of Homophile Organizations (NACHO) later that year.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Beaumont Society forms as a trans support group
description: Founded in 1966, the Beaumont Society provided information and self-help support for transgender people in Britain and remains one of the country's longest-running trans organisations.

title: The Beaumont Society is formed
description: The Beaumont Society, a support group for trans and gender-variant people, was founded in the United Kingdom.

description: The Phoenix Society for Individual Freedom in Kansas City published The Phoenix: Midwest Homophile Voice, the first LGBT magazine in the American Midwest.

title: Lilli Vincenz shows her face on The Ladder's cover
description: The January 1966 issue of The Ladder carried the first cover photograph of a lesbian with her face visible: activist Lilli Vincenz.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Senegal criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Senegal enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

title: Conti-Club opens as new Der Kreis venue in Zurich
description: On 5–6 February 1966 the Conti-Club opened at Köchlistrasse 15 in Zurich as the new club venue of Der Kreis. It served the organization until 1967 and was afterwards associated with Club 68 and the SOH.

description: Members of the Mattachine Society staged a Sip-In at Julius Bar in New York City, confronting the State Liquor Authority practice of refusing alcohol service to gay customers.

description: The 1967 CBS Reports episode The Homosexuals was the first US network television documentary to deal with homosexuality.

title: Columbia recognizes first US gay student group
description: The Student Homophile League at Columbia University became the first gay student organization in the United States to gain official institutional recognition.

title: David Hockney wins the John Moores Painting Prize
description: The artist David Hockney took the sixth John Moores Painting Prize at the Walker Art Gallery in Liverpool. Hockney became one of Britain's most celebrated openly gay artists.

sources.label: National Museums Liverpool — LGBTQ+ timeline

title: Nuestro Mundo, first gay rights group in Latin America, forms
description: Grupo Nuestro Mundo formed in Greater Buenos Aires as the first gay rights organization in Argentina — and in Latin America as a whole. Some accounts date the founding to 1969.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Oscar Wilde Bookshop opens in New York City
description: The Oscar Wilde Bookshop opened in New York City in 1967 as the world's first bookstore dedicated to homosexual-oriented literature.

title: Study treats male homosexuality as a fact of life
description: Wainwright Churchill's book Homosexual Behavior Among Males approached homosexuality scientifically as a normal variation. It introduced the term homoerotophobia, seen as a possible forerunner of the word homophobia.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Advocate begins publication
description: The Advocate started publishing in the United States and grew into a long-running LGBTQ news and culture publication, covering events from the Stonewall uprising through the AIDS crisis and marriage equality.

sources.label: Grindr for Equality — LGBTQ Rights Timeline

title: Black Cat Tavern raid sparks protests in Los Angeles
description: Twelve plainclothes officers raided the Black Cat Tavern in Silver Lake on New Year's Day 1967, beating and arresting staff and patrons. P.R.I.D.E. organized protests from 5 January — the first use of the word Pride later tied to the LGBT rights movement — which energized homosexual rights activity.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Swiss-German television airs discussions on homosexuality
description: On 30 January and 20 February 1967, German-language Swiss television broadcast discussion programs on the theme of homosexuality, framed as public education on the subject.

title: The Los Angeles Advocate publishes its first issue
description: The Advocate started in September 1967 as The Los Angeles Advocate, a local newsletter warning gay men about police raids on the city's gay bars. It later grew into a national LGBT news magazine.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Der Kreis votes to dissolve
description: At its final annual assembly on 22 October 1967, Der Kreis resolved to dissolve both its magazine and the organization itself, ending Europe's longest-running homophile publication of the postwar era.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1943-1967

title: Final issue of Der Kreis published
description: The last issue of the magazine Der Kreis appeared in December 1967, closing 35 years of continuous publication from Zurich.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1943-1967

title: Club 68 launches with its own magazine
description: On 10 December 1967 former subscribers of Der Kreis established the Club 68 association together with its German-language magazine club68, carrying the Swiss homophile movement forward after the dissolution of Der Kreis.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1943-1967

title: Bulgaria decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Bulgaria removed criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts between adults in 1968.

title: East Germany decriminalizes homosexual acts

title: National Transsexual Counseling Unit begins work
description: The National Transsexual Counseling Unit emerged in 1968 from the network of transgender social, psychological and medical support services built after the Compton's Cafeteria riot. It was the first peer-run transgender support and advocacy organization in the world.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Patch patrons answer police raid with flowers
description: After police arrested two patrons in a raid on the Los Angeles bar The Patch, owner Lee Glaze rallied the crowd, bought out a nearby flower shop, and led a caravan to the police station, where the demonstrators decorated it with flowers and bailed out the arrested men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ursus Club association forms in Bern
description: On 8 March 1968 the Ursus Club was formally constituted as an association in Bern, growing out of the private cellar gatherings that had begun in 1964.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: Bulgaria equalizes the age of consent
description: Bulgaria set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Daughters of Bilitis chapter forms in Melbourne
description: An Australian arm of the Daughters of Bilitis formed in Melbourne in 1969 and is now considered the first homosexual rights organization in Australia.

title: Gay Activists Alliance founded in New York
description: The Gay Activists Alliance formed in New York to secure basic rights and freedoms for gay people through focused political action.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

description: The Gay Liberation Front was organized in New York in the wake of the Stonewall uprising, adopting a militant approach to gay liberation.

description: The Gay Liberation Front took shape in America in 1969 in the wake of the Stonewall riots, becoming the first gay organization to carry the word gay in its name.

description: The publication begun in 1967 as the Los Angeles Advocate took the name The Advocate; it is considered the oldest continuing LGBTQ periodical in the country.

description: CAMP, the Campaign Against Moral Persecution, formed in Australia in 1970 to push for homosexual law reform and public visibility.

title: Ken Togo stands in Japanese national election
description: Openly gay activist Ken Togo ran for national office in Japan, an early moment of queer electoral visibility in the country.

description: The homosexual rights organization Society Five was founded in Melbourne, giving Victoria an early advocacy group of its own.

description: The Body Politic began publication in Toronto, growing into one of the most widely read LGBTQ rights periodicals in North America.

description: The British Gay Liberation Front was covered in the national press as a political movement, with weekly meetings drawing 200 to 300 people.

title: University of Michigan opens first collegiate LGBT programs office
description: The University of Michigan created the Gay Advocate's Office, the first office of its kind at a US college or university.

title: US Libertarian Party calls for repeal of sodomy laws
description: The newly formed Libertarian Party demanded the repeal of all victimless-crime statutes, explicitly including the sodomy laws, making it an early US party to take that position.

title: Zabriskie Release counseling service starts in Zurich
description: In 1971 Zabriskie Release, a counseling service for homosexuals, was set up in Zurich.

description: Costa Rica set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Club 68 opens short-lived St. Gallen section
description: On 20 March 1971 Club 68 established a section in St. Gallen. The local branch survived only a few months.

title: Rosa von Praunheim's landmark gay-liberation film premieres in Berlin
description: On 3 July 1971 Rosa von Praunheim's film It Is Not the Homosexual Who Is Perverse, But the Society in Which He Lives premiered in Berlin. The provocative film became a catalyst for the gay liberation movement in German-speaking countries.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: First national gay liberation protest at Parliament Hill
description: About 100 people gathered on Parliament Hill in Ottawa for Canada's first organized gay liberation demonstration, with a smaller simultaneous protest held in Vancouver.

sources.label: queerevents.ca — Canadian LGBTQ+ History Timeline

title: Seattle couple applies for same-sex marriage license
description: Faygele Ben-Miriam and Paul Barwick requested a marriage license in Seattle in September 1971. The refusal led to the 1974 court case Singer v. Hara.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Club In joins SOH as Ticino section
description: In 1972 the Club In of Lugano-Pregassona joined the Schweizerische Organisation der Homophilen, becoming the organization's Ticino section.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: Composer Angela Morley transitions and continues her BBC career
description: The composer and arranger previously known as Wally Stott, celebrated for radio comedies such as The Goons, transitioned in 1972 and, as Angela Morley, went on to an award-winning career that included conducting the BBC Concert Orchestra.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First book on gay witchcraft published

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First custody award to an openly lesbian mother in the US
description: Camille Mitchell was granted custody of her children in a divorce case, the first open lesbian in the United States to win such a ruling, though the judge barred her partner from living in the household.

description: Jim Foster of San Francisco and Madeline Davis of Buffalo spoke as the first openly gay and lesbian Democratic Convention delegates, urging a gay-rights plank in the party platform. sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hawaii repeals its sodomy law
description: The US state of Hawaii legalized consensual same-sex relations between adults by repealing its sodomy statute.

title: Jeanne Manford marches with her son, sparking PFLAG
description: Queens schoolteacher Jeanne Manford joined New York's Christopher Street Liberation Day march alongside her son, activist Morty Manford. The gesture is regarded as the starting point of the straight-ally movement and of PFLAG.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jonathan Ned Katz stages the play Coming Out
description: The documentary play Coming Out, written by Jonathan Ned Katz, was performed for the first time in New York, dramatizing gay and lesbian history.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

description: Nancy Wechsler won a seat on the Ann Arbor City Council for the Human Rights Party and came out as lesbian during her term, making her the first openly gay or lesbian person holding political office in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: National Bisexual Liberation Group forms in New York
description: The National Bisexual Liberation Group was founded in New York, an early organization dedicated specifically to bisexual visibility and community.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Norway equalizes the age of consent
description: Norway set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

title: Quakers issue Ithaca Statement on Bisexuality
description: The Committee of Friends on Bisexuality published the Ithaca Statement supporting bisexual people, printed in the Friends Journal and The Advocate. It is cited as the first statement on bisexuality by an American religious assembly.

description: The AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power, known as ACT UP, was formed in New York to press for treatment, research and legislation through direct-action protest.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: ACT UP stages its first major demonstration
description: ACT UP held its first large protest in 1987, demanding faster government action on AIDS; seventeen demonstrators were arrested.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Barney Frank becomes first US congressman to come out voluntarily
description: In 1987 Massachusetts congressman Barney Frank publicly disclosed that he is gay, the first member of the US Congress to do so of his own accord.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Boulder voters approve first US referendum banning anti-gay discrimination
description: Citizens of Boulder, Colorado passed a referendum in 1987 prohibiting discrimination based on sexual orientation, the first such ballot measure approved by voters in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: David Norris becomes first openly gay person elected to office in Ireland
description: In 1987 David Norris won election to public office in the Republic of Ireland, the country's first openly gay elected official.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First nationwide bisexual contingent marches on Washington
description: Seventy-five bisexual activists marched in the 1987 March on Washington for Gay and Lesbian Rights, the first nationwide bisexual gathering. Lani Ka'ahumanu's essay on the emerging bisexual movement appeared in the march's Civil Disobedience Handbook, the first such article carried by a national lesbian and gay publication.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First same-sex kiss in a UK television soap
description: EastEnders showed the first same-sex kiss in a British soap opera when the character Colin gave his boyfriend Barry a kiss on the forehead, prompting hostile tabloid coverage.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Hirschfeld Centre in Dublin destroyed by fire
description: The Hirschfeld Centre in Dublin was destroyed by a fire believed to have been hate-inspired arson.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Homomonument unveiled in Amsterdam
description: The Homomonument, a memorial honoring homosexuals persecuted through history, opened in Amsterdam in 1987.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: International Foundation for Gender Education founded
description: The International Foundation for Gender Education was established to advocate for and provide resources to the trans community.

sources.label: Stonewall UK — Key dates for LGBTQ+ equality

title: Irish Congress of Trade Unions publishes workplace guidelines
description: The Irish Congress of Trade Unions published Lesbian and Gay Rights in the Workplace: Guidelines for Negotiators.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Liechtenstein establishes a state AIDS support service
description: Liechtenstein sets up a state-run AIDS-Hilfe, creating an official HIV/AIDS support body for the principality.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: Manitoba adds sexual orientation to its Human Rights Code
description: Manitoba becomes the third Canadian province to include sexual orientation as a protected class under its Human Rights Code.

sources.label: UFCW Canada — LGBTQ History Timeline

title: Manitoba and Yukon adopt sexual-orientation discrimination protections
description: In 1987 the Canadian province of Manitoba and the territory of Yukon added protections against discrimination based on sexual orientation to their laws.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New York Area Bisexual Network founded
description: A group of New York bisexual activists including Brenda Howard established the New York Area Bisexual Network (NYABN) in 1987 to connect the city's bisexual community.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Princess Diana opens first specialist HIV and AIDS ward
description: Diana, Princess of Wales, opened the first specialist HIV-positive ward and pointedly shook hands with patients without wearing protective gloves or a mask, challenging stigma around the disease.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Sunil Gupta highlights suppression of gay men in India
description: Marking 40 years of Indian independence, the artist Sunil Gupta observed that the anniversary also underlined the continued suppression of gay men in India, where sexuality remained absent from public debate.

sources.label: National Museums Liverpool — LGBTQ+ timeline

title: Swiss gay youth groups form a national umbrella body
description: Gay youth groups across Switzerland create the ISJS as a national umbrella coordinating their work; the body later widened its scope to lesbian youth as the ILSJS.

title: Three more cantonal AIDS support organizations are founded
description: AIDS-Hilfe organizations are established in the cantons of Zug, Aargau and Graubünden, extending the Swiss network of regional HIV/AIDS support and prevention bodies.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: US Representative Barney Frank comes out
description: Massachusetts Congressman Barney Frank publicly came out as gay, becoming one of the first members of the US Congress to do so voluntarily while in office.

sources.label: Pride & Progress — Decades timeline

title: Youth group Albatros forms in St. Gallen
description: Albatros, a group for gay young people, is set up in St. Gallen, adding an eastern-Swiss node to the country's network of queer youth groups.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: Switzerland launches the national Stop Aids prevention campaign
description: On 3 February 1987 the national AIDS prevention campaign "Stop Aids" started, becoming one of the most visible public-health campaigns in Swiss history.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: ACT UP founded in New York
description: On 10 March 1987 the AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power, known as ACT UP, formed in New York. The group used direct action to protest drug-company profiteering and to demand faster access to treatment and stronger protections for people with AIDS.

sources.label: PBS American Experience — Milestones in the American Gay Rights Movement

title: Winnipeg holds its first official Pride Week
description: Winnipeg marked its first official Pride Week, drawing around 250 attendees.

sources.label: queerevents.ca — Canadian LGBTQ+ History Timeline

description: In August 1987 the Swiss gay youth group Spot25 began publishing spot-news, a bulletin appearing every two months.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: 600,000 march for lesbian and gay rights in Washington
description: On 11 October 1987 some 600,000 people marched in Washington, D.C. for lesbian and gay rights; the date's anniversary was later established as National Coming Out Day.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: Second National March on Washington demands AIDS action
description: On 11 October 1987 hundreds of thousands joined a National March on Washington, pressing President Ronald Reagan to confront the AIDS crisis and expand support for those affected.

title: National gay demonstration held in Bern instead of a CSD
description: On 17 October 1987 no Christopher Street Day took place in Switzerland; a national gay demonstration in Bern was staged in its place.

title: ILGA holds its world conference in Zurich
description: From 28 to 30 December 1987 the ILGA world conference met in Zurich, organized jointly by the Swiss groups SOH, HACH and HAZ.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: Belize listed as decriminalizing sex between men
description: The source timeline records Belize as removing criminal penalties for sexual acts between men in 1988. Other accounts date Belize's decriminalization to a 2016 court ruling, so the 1988 claim requires verification.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Canada lowers the age of consent for sodomy to 18
description: Canadian law was amended in 1988 to reduce the age of consent for sodomy to 18, easing but not fully equalizing the rules applying to same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Canadian MP Svend Robinson comes out
description: In 1988 Svend Robinson publicly came out, becoming Canada's first openly gay Member of Parliament.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dialogai in Geneva adds two member groups
description: The Geneva organization Dialogai gains a motorcycle club, Gaymotards, together with a Christian group, chrétiens et homosexuel-les (C+H).

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: European Court of Human Rights strikes down Irish sodomy law in Norris v. Ireland
description: After losing before the Irish High Court in 1980 and the Irish Supreme Court in 1983, David Norris brought his case to the European Court of Human Rights, which ruled in 1988 that Ireland's criminalization of sex between men violated the right to privacy. Irish law nonetheless kept homosexuality illegal until 1993.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: European court rules in Norris v Ireland
description: David Norris lost his domestic case for same-sex rights, but the European Court of Human Rights case Norris v Ireland paved the way for the later decriminalisation of homosexuality in Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Gay and Lesbian Equality Network founded in Ireland
description: The Gay and Lesbian Equality Network was founded by Don Donnelly, Charles Kerrigan, Suzy Byrne, Kieran Rose and Christopher Robson to change legislation and social policy toward full equality and protection for LGBT people in Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Gay community groups organize in Neuchâtel
description: Homologay forms in Neuchâtel as a gay association paired with an anti-AIDS association, alongside a new les-bi-gay group; the independent Groupe Sida Neuchâtel starts the same year.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1967-1989

title: Germany equalizes the age of consent
description: Germany set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Guinea criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Guinea enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Halifax holds its first official Pride March
description: Halifax's first official Pride March drew about 75 participants.

sources.label: queerevents.ca — Canadian LGBTQ+ History Timeline

title: Ian McKellen comes out in response to Section 28
description: Actor Ian McKellen came out publicly on BBC radio while opposing Section 28, becoming a prominent voice for LGBTQ+ rights.

sources.label: Stonewall UK — Key dates for LGBTQ+ equality

title: Israel repeals its ban on sex between men
description: In 1988 Israel formally decriminalized sexual acts between men by repealing a provision inherited from the 1936 British mandate code, a law the state had never actually enforced.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kiss-in protest outside Leinster House in Dublin
description: Activists staged a kiss-in outside Leinster House in Dublin at a time when sexual activity between men was still illegal, taking a considerable personal risk. A later protest was held outside the Vatican Embassy.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Robert Dover becomes the first openly gay Olympic athlete
description: American equestrian Robert Dover competed as the first openly gay athlete at the Olympic Games in 1988.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Stacy Offner becomes first openly lesbian rabbi at a mainstream congregation
description: In 1988 the Reform congregation Shir Tikvah of Minneapolis hired Stacy Offner, the first openly lesbian rabbi employed by a mainstream Jewish congregation.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Stonewall UK is founded in response to Section 28
description: The campaigning group Stonewall was set up in 1988 by Ian McKellen, Lisa Power and Michael Cashman, among others, to oppose Section 28 and lobby for LGBTQ+ equality.

title: Sweden passes pioneering legal protections for same-sex couples
description: In 1988 Sweden became the first country to adopt laws protecting homosexuals in matters of social services, taxation, and inheritance.

title: Sydney Mardi Gras adds lesbians to its name

description: The World Health Organization and the UK Government co-hosted the World AIDS Summit in London, the first time nations gathered to discuss AIDS, drawing health ministers from 148 countries. The London Declaration on AIDS Prevention protected individuals from discrimination in prevention programmes.

description: During a Radio 3 debate about Section 28, the actor Ian McKellen came out publicly. He went on to help found the LGBT rights charity Stonewall.

description: Canada began granting refugee status to homosexuals who feared for their safety in their home countries, recognizing persecution based on sexual orientation as grounds for protection. The source timeline lists this under 1994.

title: Capricorn group forms for gays and lesbians in Graubünden
description: Capricorn, a group for gays and lesbians in the canton of Graubünden, came into being, organizing community life in the mountain canton.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Congressman Steve Gunderson outed
description: Wisconsin Congressman Steve Gunderson was publicly outed as gay while serving in the US House, making him one of the few openly gay Republicans in Congress at the time.

sources.label: Pride & Progress — Decades timeline

title: Deborah Batts becomes the first openly gay US federal judge
description: Deborah Batts was appointed to the US District Court in New York in 1994, making her the first openly gay or lesbian federal judge in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First national UK radio news magazine for gay listeners launches
description: 'Out This Week' began on BBC Radio Five Live, the first national radio news magazine made for lesbians and gay men. Presenter Nigel Wrench later used a Sony Award speech to come out as HIV positive.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Isle of Man fully decriminalises homosexuality
description: The Isle of Man completed the decriminalisation of consensual same-sex relations on the island.

sources.label: Stonewall UK — Key dates for LGBTQ+ equality

title: Israel begins recognizing unregistered cohabitation for same-sex couples
description: In 1994 Israel started extending unregistered-cohabitation recognition to same-sex couples, granting partner benefits without a formal partnership registry. Adoption remained excluded, with step-adoption following only in 2005.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Italian-speaking gay community group CGST forms
description: The Comunità Gay Svizzera di Lingua Italiana (CGST) was created to serve gay men in Ticino and the Italian-speaking southern valleys of Graubünden.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Kosovo decriminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Kosovo repealed the laws that criminalized consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, ending the legal prohibition on homosexuality.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Olympic diver Greg Louganis comes out publicly
description: The four-time Olympic gold medal diver Greg Louganis publicly came out as gay, later recounting his life in the 1996 autobiography Breaking the Surface.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Pedro Zamora's commitment ceremony airs as the first same-sex ceremony on television
description: MTV's The Real World broadcast the commitment ceremony of Pedro Zamora and Sean Sasser in 1994, showing the couple exchanging vows. It was the first same-sex ceremony shown on television, a milestone in the medium's history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Queer Asian American groups join San Francisco's Chinese New Year Parade
description: The Gay Asian Pacific Alliance and Asian Pacific Sister marched in San Francisco's Chinese New Year Parade in 1994 — the first public participation of openly queer Asian American communities in an ethnic community event of this kind.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sandi Toksvig comes out as lesbian
description: Broadcaster Sandi Toksvig came out publicly as a lesbian, an early high-profile coming out in British public life.

sources.label: Stonewall UK — Key dates for LGBTQ+ equality

title: Serbia decriminalizes male same-sex acts
description: Serbia removed the criminal ban on consensual sex between men from its penal code in 1994.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Sheila Kuehl elected to the California Assembly
description: Sheila Kuehl won election to the California State Assembly, becoming the first openly gay or lesbian person elected to the California Legislature.

sources.label: Pride & Progress — Decades timeline

title: South Africa's interim constitution protects against sexual-orientation discrimination
description: South Africa's interim constitution, in force from 1994, included sexual orientation among the grounds protected from discrimination — the first national constitution in the world to do so. The protection carried into the final constitution of 1996.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: South African activists launch the National Coalition for Gay and Lesbian Equality
description: The National Coalition for Gay and Lesbian Equality formed in South Africa in 1994 as a national umbrella body. It went on to defend the sexual-orientation clause in the constitution and to litigate key equality cases.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Spazio Gay Ticino opens as a meeting place near Lugano
description: The Spazio Gay Ticino opened its doors in Massagno near Lugano, giving the region's gay community a dedicated meeting place.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Steve Gunderson is outed on the House floor
description: US Representative Steve Gunderson was outed as gay on the House floor in 1994 by Representative Bob Dornan during a debate on federal funding for gay-friendly school curricula. The outing made him one of the first openly gay members of Congress and the first openly gay Republican representative.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: TGWU publishes Pride Over Prejudice leaflet
description: The TGWU published the Pride Over Prejudice leaflet supporting LGBT members, who began self-organising and addressing workplace issues such as equal benefits for same-sex partners.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: The American Medical Association stops treating homosexuality as an illness
description: In 1994 the American Medical Association removed language classifying homosexuality as an illness from its policies, aligning the organization with the earlier declassification by American psychiatry.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The US recognizes fear of anti-gay persecution as grounds for asylum
description: In 1994 the United States recognized fear of persecution based on sexual orientation as a valid basis for asylum, opening protection to LGBTQ people fleeing hostile countries.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Tim Van Zandt elected to the Missouri House
description: Tim Van Zandt won a seat in the Missouri House of Representatives as an openly gay candidate, an early milestone for LGBTQ+ representation in the state.

sources.label: Pride & Progress — Decades timeline

title: Wilson Cruz becomes the first gay actor in a leading openly gay TV role
description: In 1994 Wilson Cruz played Rickie Vasquez on My So-Called Life, becoming the first openly gay actor to portray an openly gay character in a leading role on a television series.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Montreal's Katakombes bar raided by police
description: Police raided the Katakombes bar in Montreal and arrested 165 men.

sources.label: queerevents.ca — Canadian LGBTQ+ History Timeline

title: Filmmaker Derek Jarman dies of an AIDS-related illness
description: The filmmaker, artist and activist Derek Jarman died from an AIDS-related illness. His work and openness about his diagnosis made him a prominent figure in British queer culture.

sources.label: National Museums Liverpool — LGBTQ+ timeline

title: The UN Human Rights Committee rules against Tasmania's sodomy laws in Toonen v. Australia
description: In 1994 the UN Human Rights Committee decided Toonen v. Australia, finding that Tasmania's criminalization of consensual sex between men violated the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights. It was the first time a UN body held that sexual-orientation discrimination breached international human-rights law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Belarus equalizes the age of consent
description: Belarus set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Petitpierre asks the Swiss government about equal rights for same-sex couples
description: Council of States member Gilles Petitpierre (FDP, Geneva) submitted a parliamentary question to the Federal Council asking what was being done about equal rights for same-sex couples. The query is regarded as the starting impulse for Switzerland's registered-partnership law.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Zurich marks 25 years of Stonewall with a two-month festival
description: An organizing committee in Zurich staged a festival program commemorating the 25th anniversary of the Stonewall riots. The celebrations revived the city's Christopher Street Day events, which had lapsed for several years.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Geneva holds a gay pride under the slogan 'Personne n'est parfait'
description: A gay pride march took place in Geneva on 25 June 1994, carrying the slogan 'Personne n'est parfait' (Nobody is perfect).

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Prince Edward Island holds its first pride march
description: Prince Edward Island held its first gay and lesbian pride march.

sources.label: queerevents.ca — Canadian LGBTQ+ History Timeline

title: Tokyo holds its first gay pride parade
description: A gay pride parade took place in Tokyo in August 1994, the first march of its kind in Japan.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rodney Wilson launches the world's first LGBTQ History Month
description: Missouri teacher Rodney Charles Wilson initiated the first LGBTQ History Month in the United States in October 1994, creating an annual observance dedicated to queer history that later spread to other countries.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Swiss homophile organization SOH votes to dissolve
description: An extraordinary general assembly of the Schweizerische Organisation der Homophilen (SOH) resolved to dissolve the organization and asked its members to join the national association Pink Cross instead.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Yemen criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Yemen enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Aargay group forms in Baden
description: Gay men in Baden organized themselves as the Aargay, creating a community group for the canton of Aargau.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: ABC televises the Sydney Mardi Gras
description: Despite public criticism of its plans, the ABC broadcast the Sydney Mardi Gras on national television for the first time.

sources.label: National Museum of Australia — LGBTI history interactive timeline 1975–2019

title: Albania decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: Albania repealed its criminal ban on consensual same-sex acts in 1995, ending the prohibition inherited from the communist-era penal code.

title: Antigua and Barbuda criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Antigua and Barbuda enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Cantonal AIDS organization Empreinte established in Fribourg
description: Empreinte was set up as the cantonal AIDS support organization for Fribourg. From 1996 it cooperated with the local gay group Sarigai.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Dialogai starts a group for gay retirees in Geneva
description: The Geneva organization Dialogai created 'Gays retraités', a group serving retired gay men.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Ed Flanagan becomes the first openly gay statewide-elected official in the US
description: Vermont State Auditor Ed Flanagan came out as gay in 1995, making him the first openly gay official elected statewide in the United States. He won reelection in 1996 and served through 2001.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay Advice Darlington/Durham forms in north-east England
description: Local gay and bisexual men set up Gay Advice Darlington/Durham in 1995. The group grew into a charity serving the LGBT community of County Durham and Darlington.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay and Lesbian Association of Doctors and Dentists is founded
description: The Gay and Lesbian Association of Doctors and Dentists (GLADD) was established in the United Kingdom. Its work included the Dignity at Work document and a report on support for healthcare professionals living with HIV.

sources.label: Royal Society of Medicine — Snapshots in LGBTQ+ Medical History

title: Gay swim club Aquarius forms in Lausanne
description: A gay swimming club in Lausanne adopted the name 'Aquarius, club de natation', adding an organized sports group to the city's community.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Gaytime TV launches as the BBC's first LGBT television series
description: 'Gaytime TV' began on BBC Two, the first BBC television programme aimed at an LGBT audience, presented by Rhona Cameron, Bert Tyler-Moore and Richard Fairbrass. It ran until 1999.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: HAZ offers a coming-out group for gay fathers
description: The Zurich organization HAZ began offering a coming-out support group aimed at gay fathers.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: HAZ starts a group for gay men over 25 in Zurich
description: The Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Zürich (HAZ) set up a new group for gay men over the age of 25.

sources.label: schwulengeschichte.ch — Zeittafel 1990-1999

title: Mermaids founded to support trans youth
description: Parents of gender-variant children founded Mermaids to support trans and gender-diverse young people and their families.

title: Michael Nelson elected mayor of Carrboro
description: Michael Nelson was elected mayor of Carrboro, North Carolina, becoming one of the first openly gay mayors in the state.

title: Moldova decriminalizes same-sex acts
description: Moldova removed the criminal ban on consensual sex between men from its penal code in 1995.

title: Newfoundland and Labrador bans discrimination based on sexual orientation
description: The Canadian province of Newfoundland and Labrador added sexual orientation to its human-rights protections in 1995.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rabbi Margaret Wenig publishes the first Jewish case for gay civil marriage
description: Rabbi Margaret Wenig's essay Truly Welcoming Lesbian and Gay Jews appeared in 1995 in a volume honoring Rabbi Alexander M. Schindler. It was the first published argument addressed to the Jewish community in favor of civil marriage for gay couples.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rachel Maddow wins a Rhodes Scholarship as the first openly gay American recipient
description: Rachel Maddow received a Rhodes Scholarship in 1995, becoming the first openly gay or lesbian American to win the international award.

title: Roberta Achtenberg runs for San Francisco mayor
description: Roberta Achtenberg mounted an openly lesbian campaign for mayor of San Francisco, one of the highest-profile LGBTQ+ big-city mayoral bids of the decade.

title: Spain equalizes the age of consent
description: Spain set an equal age of consent regardless of sexual orientation, removing the higher threshold that had applied to same-sex relations.

sources.label: Equaldex — LGBT Rights Timeline

title: Sri Lanka criminalizes same-sex sexual activity
description: Sri Lanka enacted a legal prohibition on consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults, making homosexuality a punishable offense.
